pattern: The set of valid left endpoints for a fixed right endpoint only ever grows monotonically as the window slides (classic max-min-within-k two-pointer), so instead of re-scanning every valid left endpoint for the best XOR, maintain a binary trie of prefix-XOR values for exactly the currently-valid left endpoints and query it for the best XOR partner in O(bits) as the window slides.
hints: First handle the max-min-at-most-k restriction on its own: as the right end of the subarray advances, the smallest valid left end is monotonically non-decreasing, so a standard two-pointer sliding window (with a max-deque and a min-deque tracking the current window's extremes) tells you, for every right endpoint, the full contiguous range of valid left endpoints.

Rewrite subarray XOR using prefix XORs: XOR(l, r) = prefix[r+1] XOR prefix[l]. For a fixed r, you need the prefix[l] (over all valid l in the current window) that maximizes the XOR against prefix[r+1] — that is exactly the classic 'maximum XOR with a given number' query, answerable in O(bits) with a binary trie built from the most significant bit down.

Because the window of valid l's slides forward (old lefts drop out, new lefts join) rather than being rebuilt from scratch, keep ONE trie with per-node counts so you can both insert prefix[r] as r advances and remove prefix[l] as the window's left edge advances, instead of rebuilding the trie for every r.

edge_inputs.description: single element — the only subarray is the element itself
single element at the maximum possible value, with k=0

k=0 forces every valid subarray to be constant-valued; the best constrained answer (9, a lone large element) is actually BIGGER than the unrestricted whole-array XOR (9^1^9=1), proving the range restriction must genuinely gate which subarrays are considered rather than just computing a global max-subarray-XOR
k=0 with a long run of one repeated value interrupted by a single larger outlier — the best answer is the odd-one-out element itself (7), which beats any odd-length run of the repeated value (3)
all elements identical with k=0 — every single element (or any odd-length run) gives the same value; XOR of an even-length run of equal values collapses to 0
k large enough that the whole array is always one valid window (no restriction in practice) — reduces to the unrestricted maximum subarray XOR
k=0 on a strictly increasing array (all distinct) — every valid subarray is a single element, so the answer is just the maximum element

stress.description: random arrays up to length 4000 with random values and random k, seeded, exercising many window shapes
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, size)
    nums = [rng.randint(0, 32767) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(0, 32767)
    return [nums, k]
